did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep learning model called CFLDnet (based on DenseNet with Convolutional Block Attention Module) for dermoscopic image melanoma recognition. It employs Sample Focal Loss to handle class imbalance and achieves an average recognition accuracy of 86.89% on the ISIC2018 task3 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel deep learning model called CFLDnet for dermoscopic image melanoma recognition. It details the architecture (CBA DenseNet), introduces a specific loss function (Sample Focal Loss), describes experimental settings using the ISIC2018 dataset, and reports new performance results (86.89% accuracy) compared to baseline models.

title: Dermatoscopic image melanoma recognition based on CFLDnet fusion network
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenges of class imbalance and high similarity between classes in dermoscopic melanoma recognition by proposing a CFLDnet fusion network. The model integrates an improved Convolutional Block Attention (CBA) module into DenseNet to enhance feature extraction while utilizing Sample Focal Loss to mitigate data imbalance. Experiments were conducted on the ISIC2018 task 3 dataset, which contains 10,015 images across seven lesion categories, using five-fold cross-validation. The proposed CFLDnet achieved an average recognition accuracy of 86.89%, outperforming baseline models such as VGG16, ResNet50, InceptionV3, and standard DenseNet121. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of combining attention mechanisms with focal loss for improving classification performance in imbalanced dermatological datasets.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven lesion categories in the ISIC2018 task 3 dataset used for classification. Count: 1113 images (11.11%).

all_task_types.target: Seven lesion categories (MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC) in dermoscopic images
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a CFLDnet fusion network for dermoscopic image lesion classification. It addresses class imbalance in the ISIC2018 task 3 dataset, which contains seven types of skin lesions. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) using Sample Focal Loss. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with an average recognition accuracy of 86.89%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2018 Task 3
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopy image dataset released by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains 10,015 skin pathology images (600x450 RGB) divided into seven lesion categories. The study utilized this dataset for classification experiments, splitting it into a training set of 8,012 images and a test set of 2,003 images, and employed five-fold cross-validation.

use.training: Used for training the CFLDnet model via five-fold cross-validation (8012 images total across folds).
use.validation: Used for validation within the five-fold cross-validation scheme.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation (2003 images).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To verify the effectiveness of the proposed method and take the average value for comparison due to severe imbalance of different pathological types in the dataset.
To evaluate and compare the performance of the CFLDnet model against baseline models (VGG16, ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet121) and different loss functions.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using average recognition accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score derived from five-fold cross-validation without statistical significance testing.

ISIC2018 Task 3.description: Dataset containing 10,015 skin pathology images divided into seven lesion categories (MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC). Split into 8,012 training images and 2,003 test images. Evaluated using five-fold cross-validation.

Baseline Network Architectures (with Cro.description: Comparison of VGG16, ResNet50, InceptionV3, and DenseNet121 using standard cross-entropy (CE) loss.
Baseline Network Architectures (with Cro.notes: ResNet50 and DenseNet121 achieved better performance than VGG16 and InceptionV3. DenseNet121 was chosen as the leading network due to fewer parameters and better feature reuse.

CBA DenseNet Variants (with Focal Loss).description: Comparison of three CBA DenseNet variants (d121, d121_c, d121_t, d121_c_t) using Focal Loss (FL). d121_c performed best.
CBA DenseNet Variants (with Focal Loss).notes: d121_c had the highest accuracy and lowest loss among variants. Accuracy increased by 1.81% and recall by 4.26% compared to standard DenseNet121 with CE loss.

Loss Function Comparison (on CBA DenseNe.description: Comparison of Cross-Entropy (CE), Focal Loss (FL), and Sample Focal Loss (SFL) on the CBA DenseNet model (referred to as CM_D121 in Table 5).
Loss Function Comparison (on CBA DenseNe.notes: SFL improved accuracy by 0.80% over FL and 2.53% over CE. The average recognition accuracy of the seven types of lesions was 86.60% (per confusion matrix analysis) or 86.89% (overall accuracy). F1-score reached 83.06%.

Per-Class Accuracy (CFLDnet).description: Recognition accuracy for each of the seven lesion types using the proposed CFLDnet model.
Per-Class Accuracy (CFLDnet).notes: AKIEC and NV had the highest recognition accuracy. MEL and BKL had the poorest recognition accuracy due to class imbalance and similarity.

Comparison with Other Methods.description: Comparison of the proposed CFLDnet with other existing research methods on the same dataset.
Comparison with Other Methods.notes: The proposed CFLDnet achieved 86.89% accuracy. It was lower than SE-ResNet101 (87.20%) but higher than other methods using similar or no data augmentation.

ISIC2018 Task 3.notes: The paper reports that the CFLDnet model achieved an average recognition accuracy of 86.89%. The confusion matrix analysis showed an average recognition accuracy of 86.60% across the seven lesion types. The model outperformed baseline networks (VGG16, ResNet50, InceptionV3, DenseNet121) and other comparative methods when using comparable data augmentation (random erasing).

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning with weights initialized from ImageNet-trained DenseNet121

architecture_details.cbam_placement: Integrated after the last composite module of each densely connected block (variant d121_c)
architecture_details.cbam_structure: Channel attention module (using max and average pooling, MLP with reduction rate r=16) and spatial attention module
variants_compared: d121_c_t (CBAM after both composite and transition layers)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Ensemble of multiple models + random forest classifier
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Original images (600x450) were uniformly adjusted to 224x224 along the shortest edge, preserving aspect ratio and original shape/structure information.
Normalization: Per-channel-mean normalization was applied by subtracting the channel average intensity value calculated on each single image to standardize illumination and skin color differences.
Data Augmentation: Random erasing was used as the data augmentation technique.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used as the primary data augmentation method for the proposed CFLDnet model and compared baseline methods (CNN + handcrafted feature + SVM, Ensemble of multiple models + random forest classifier). Specific probabilities or erasing ratios were not reported in the text.
Mentioned as traditional data expansion strategies used to expand the original dataset, though specific implementation details or probabilities were not provided.

Used by the comparative method SE-ResNet101 + Mean teacher [22]. 'Body hair' likely refers to hair removal or augmentation related to hair artifacts, and 'Between-class learning' refers to a specific learning strategy rather than a standard geometric/color transform.

Used by the comparative method Inception-V4 + Inception-Resnet-v2 [12]. Likely refers to normalization techniques rather than generative augmentation, but listed under data augmentation in the comparison table.

conclusions: The proposed CFLDnet fusion network, which integrates an improved Convolutional Block Attention (CBA) module into DenseNet and utilizes Sample Focal Loss (SFL), effectively addresses the challenges of class imbalance and high similarity between classes in dermoscopic melanoma recognition.

The CFLDnet model achieved an average recognition accuracy of 86.89% on the ISIC2018 task 3 dataset, outperforming baseline models such as VGG16, ResNet50, InceptionV3, and standard DenseNet121 with cross-entropy loss.

Among the CBA DenseNet variants tested, placing the attention module after the last composite module (d121_c) yielded the highest performance, improving accuracy by 1.81% and recall by 4.26% compared to standard DenseNet121, with a computationally effective increase in parameters and training time.

The use of Sample Focal Loss (SFL) significantly improved classification performance over standard Cross-Entropy loss and standard Focal Loss, particularly in handling the severe class imbalance present in the dataset.

While the proposed CFLDnet achieved high accuracy (86.89%), it was slightly lower than the SE-ResNet101 + Mean teacher method (87.20%); however, it demonstrated superior resource efficiency compared to methods with similar data augmentation strategies.
The study confirms that combining attention mechanisms with focal loss functions is an effective strategy for improving classification performance in imbalanced dermatological datasets.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; results are confined to the ISIC2018 Task 3 dataset, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The dataset exhibits severe class imbalance (e.g., NV: 66.95% vs. DF: 1.15%), which may bias the model towards majority classes despite the use of Sample Focal Loss.
No formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance comparisons rely solely on point estimates of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

The proposed CFLDnet achieved 86.89% accuracy, which is lower than the SE-ResNet101 + Mean teacher method (87.20%) reported in comparative experiments, though the latter used more complex augmentation strategies.
Melanoma (MEL) recognition accuracy was relatively low (62.9%), indicating potential difficulties in distinguishing melanoma from benign lesions like Nevus and Benign Keratosis.
Data leakage risks exist if the five-fold cross-validation splits were not strictly patient-level independent, though the paper does not explicitly confirm this.
The model's clinical relevance is limited by its application to only seven specific lesion categories in a single public dataset, without testing on multi-class or real-world clinical workflows.
Computational efficiency is claimed as a strength (7.69M parameters), but resource usage comparisons with other methods are not fully detailed beyond parameter counts.
